caveats: a: The occupation mix is for a broader four-digit industry dominated by crop laborers who may work outside this code.
a: Guidance systems already automate steering on much row-crop acreage, so their existing benefit is excluded from remaining exposure.
a: Exposure differs between row-crop ground work, specialty crops, aerial application, pollination, and labor-intensive transplanting or thinning.
rho: Farm adoption statistics are not implementation rates among external contractors.
rho: Near-total steering automation does not eliminate operators responsible for implements, roads, refills, obstacles, and failures.
rho: Adoption is concentrated in larger row-crop operations and transfers less directly to small fields and specialty crops.
e: Multi-service agricultural contractors can change NAICS classification as harvesting, labor, postharvest, or farm-management revenue changes.
e: Some custom operations are side businesses of farms and may be captive in substance or lack separable financials.
e: The provided firm count is margin-bridged and sensitive to the low assumed industry margin.
s5: The Census age evidence is cross-industry, represents responding employer-business owners, and uses 2018 data.
s5: Equipment and customer lists can transfer without acquisition of the legal operating company.
s5: Owner age is not a direct measure of sale intent, timing, or control-transfer type.
q: One regional survey cannot represent all crops, geographies, field sizes, equipment, or application risks.
q: Published rates are self-reported and often exclude materials, while actual contracts may bundle them.
q: Retention differs between commodity row-crop work, specialty crops, aerial spraying, and precision prescription services.
d5: Long-run broad-sector receipt growth does not isolate soil preparation, planting, and cultivating.
d5: National cropland can be stable while regional weather and crop shifts cause severe local demand volatility.
d5: Constant-quality adjustment is difficult because precision documentation and variable-rate application add service content per acre.
o: Technology can strengthen contractors through utilization scale or disintermediate them by making self-performance easier.
o: The accountable operator may shift to an equipment dealer, input retailer, cooperative, or farm employee rather than disappear.
o: Operator-required share varies by field size, local labor, customer capital, crop, and equipment specialization.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Customers are crop farms, landowners, farm managers, cooperatives, and agribusinesses that outsource equipment-intensive field passes. Revenue is commonly charged per acre, per hour, per job, or by application volume, with materials sometimes billed separately; repeat demand follows planting, tillage, fertilizer, spraying, and cultivation calendars.
lens.excluded_activities: Shells, captive internal farm units, non-control financing vehicles, passive equipment lessors without accountable operations, crop harvesting, postharvest services, farm-labor contractors, full farm-management services, input retail without field application, farms self-performing work, firms outside the screened EBITDA band, and operations that lack recurring external customers are excluded.
lens.included_activities: US lower-middle-market contractors that repeatedly provide soil preparation, plowing, seedbed preparation, fertilizing, seeding, planting, transplanting, thinning, pollinating, cultivating, crop spraying, and weed or pest-control field services to external farms while retaining accountable responsibility for equipment, operators, scheduling, application accuracy, safety, compliance, and job completion.

a.bridge: BLS measures occupations across all NAICS 1151 crop-production support activities, including labor contracting, harvesting, and postharvest work, rather than wage-weighted current unautomated tasks in NAICS 115112. USDA separately measures precision-guidance adoption and technical capabilities on customer farms, not contractor labor. I translate the broader heavy agricultural-worker mix and demonstrated automated steering into exposure for equipment operation, routing, prescription execution, scouting, scheduling, quoting, documentation, and dispatch, while discounting setup, road transport, loading, repairs, chemical handling, field exceptions, customer coordination, and work already automated. The broad OEWS population likely overstates manual crop labor and understates equipment-operator exposure in this code.
caveats: The occupation mix is for a broader four-digit industry dominated by crop laborers who may work outside this code.
Guidance systems already automate steering on much row-crop acreage, so their existing benefit is excluded from remaining exposure.
Exposure differs between row-crop ground work, specialty crops, aerial application, pollination, and labor-intensive transplanting or thinning.
a.change_evidence: Obtain NAICS 115112 payroll by occupation and run wage-weighted time-and-task studies at at least twenty screened contractors across tillage, planting, fertilizer, aerial and ground spraying, and cultivation, explicitly measuring current automation, operator interventions, travel, setup, maintenance, office work, and avoided hiring.
a.rationale: AI and autonomy can reduce in-cab attention, optimize field paths and input rates, supervise multiple machines, detect skips and overlaps, produce application records, schedule around weather, route crews, estimate jobs, and handle office workflows. Physical implement changes, refilling, calibration checks, repairs, transport, stuck equipment, obstacle response, chemical stewardship, and accountable field decisions remain human and equipment dependent.

d5.bridge: USDA reports long-run real receipts for the broad agricultural-services sector and national farm and cropland trends, not constant-price, constant-quality demand for the current NAICS 115112 service basket five years ahead. I combine increasing reliance on service establishments with modest contraction in farms and harvested cropland, widening for farm consolidation, cover crops, application intensity, input prices, weather, crop mix, and customer insourcing. Broad support-service growth likely overstates this exact code, while acreage alone understates additional passes and higher-precision services.
caveats: Long-run broad-sector receipt growth does not isolate soil preparation, planting, and cultivating.
National cropland can be stable while regional weather and crop shifts cause severe local demand volatility.
Constant-quality adjustment is difficult because precision documentation and variable-rate application add service content per acre.
d5.change_evidence: Build a constant-quality demand index from contractor acres and passes by service, USDA planted and harvested acreage, cover-crop acres, tillage practices, pesticide and fertilizer applications, custom-work expense, farm-size distribution, and surveyed outsourcing versus self-performance.
d5.rationale: Most planted acres still need some combination of soil preparation, seeding, fertilizer, crop protection, or cultivation, and farms have increasingly used support providers. Total farm and harvested-cropland counts have declined, limiting pure acreage growth, while farm scale, specialized equipment, labor scarcity, precision requirements, and cover-crop adoption can increase outsourcing or passes per acre. Reduced tillage, input consolidation, drought, and customer equipment ownership can offset these drivers.

caveats: Multi-service agricultural contractors can change NAICS classification as harvesting, labor, postharvest, or farm-management revenue changes.
Some custom operations are side businesses of farms and may be captive in substance or lack separable financials.
The provided firm count is margin-bridged and sensitive to the low assumed industry margin.
e.change_evidence: Build a firm-level universe from Economic Census microdata and commercial registries, then verify external-customer revenue, service mix, repeat acreage, normalized EBITDA, active equipment and employees, related-farm ownership, and whether materials or equipment sales dominate receipts.
e.rationale: The industry definition is itself a set of repeatable external crop-production services, so most active firms in the lower-middle-market band should fit. Exclusions remain for captive divisions, passive equipment rental, input sellers whose application is incidental, firms with predominantly harvesting or labor-contracting revenue, one-off land improvement projects, and entities whose apparent receipts do not support normalized EBITDA in the band.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 115112 ($1,564,103,303) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 115112 ($4,597,652,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.0804 (margins.json[11]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Farming/Agriculture EBITDA/Sales 8.04%); revenue/employee $388,282 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 115112); class <5: 1,507 firms, avg revenue/firm $757,036 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $60,866 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 348 firms, avg revenue/firm $2,322,638 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $186,740 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 161 firms, avg revenue/firm $4,651,658 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $373,993 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 98 firms, avg revenue/firm $12,506,051 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $1,005,487 -> IN $1-10M band; class 100-499: 22 firms, avg revenue/firm $27,129,636 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $2,181,223 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 6 firms, avg revenue/firm $12,859,667 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $1,033,917 -> IN $1-10M band; n_band = 126

caveats: Technology can strengthen contractors through utilization scale or disintermediate them by making self-performance easier.
The accountable operator may shift to an equipment dealer, input retailer, cooperative, or farm employee rather than disappear.
Operator-required share varies by field size, local labor, customer capital, crop, and equipment specialization.
o.change_evidence: Measure acres shifted between custom contractors and self-performance, machinery purchases and sharing, autonomous-equipment adoption, dealer service models, contractor exits, remote-supervision staffing, input-retailer bundling, and outsourcing rates by farm size and crop.
o.rationale: Field operations still require accountable equipment deployment, agronomic prescriptions, safe application, and completion even when machines steer or operate autonomously. However, customer farms can buy or retrofit equipment, subscribe to guidance, share machinery, or assign work to employees, eliminating the independent supplier from the service basket. High equipment cost, narrow seasonal windows, labor scarcity, utilization economics, and specialized compliance preserve outsourcing for many customers.

q.bridge: University extension custom-rate surveys observe per-acre market charges and their dispersion for tillage, cultivating, and planting in selected states, not the discounted share of an AI-enabled gross benefit retained nationwide. I infer that job pricing allows near-term retention between annual resets, then discount for transparent local benchmarks, competitive bidding, customer insourcing, fuel and equipment cost changes, and renewal negotiation. Regional survey rates may overstate national price transparency but understate differentiation for timely, precise, documented work.
caveats: One regional survey cannot represent all crops, geographies, field sizes, equipment, or application risks.
Published rates are self-reported and often exclude materials, while actual contracts may bundle them.
Retention differs between commodity row-crop work, specialty crops, aerial spraying, and precision prescription services.
q.change_evidence: Review customer contracts, invoices, acre-level job costs, fuel and material pass-throughs, annual price resets, bids, utilization, rework, on-time premiums, lost jobs, and customer insourcing before and after precision or autonomous deployment across representative regions.
q.rationale: Per-acre and per-job prices let contractors keep part of lower labor or rework cost in the current season, especially when timeliness, precision, scarce capacity, and trusted compliance differentiate service. Annual custom-rate publications, neighboring operators, machinery ownership alternatives, and sophisticated farms expose costs and pressure price. Savings may also be shared through greater scheduling flexibility or added data rather than lower rates.

rho.bridge: USDA observes high adoption of auto-steer, guidance, mapping, and soil or yield data on crop farms, not five-year implementation of exposed AI labor opportunity at custom-service firms. I translate demonstrated field automation and large-farm adoption toward contractor implementation, then discount for mixed equipment fleets, retrofit cost, subscriptions, field connectivity, terrain, safety, public-road movement, liability, customer data permissions, chemical rules, seasonal payback, and human exception handling. Contractors can spread technology cost across more acres than a single farm, biasing implementation upward relative to average farms, but customer-farm adoption does not prove contractor adoption.
caveats: Farm adoption statistics are not implementation rates among external contractors.
Near-total steering automation does not eliminate operators responsible for implements, roads, refills, obstacles, and failures.
Adoption is concentrated in larger row-crop operations and transfers less directly to small fields and specialty crops.
rho.change_evidence: Survey screened contractors and equipment vendors on installed autonomy, acres and tasks covered, remote-supervision ratios, operator interventions, downtime, insurance acceptance, safety events, customer consent, capital cost, utilization, subscription cost, and realized labor hours over consecutive seasons.
rho.rationale: Machine guidance is already mature and lowers the technical barrier to path planning, multi-machine supervision, and precision documentation. Contractors have strong incentives to increase acres per operator during narrow weather windows. Fully unattended work is slower because services cross many farms, fields, crops, implements, road routes, prescriptions, and unexpected conditions, and customers require accountable execution.

s5.bridge: Census measures owner ages across responding US employer businesses in 2018, not qualifying control transfers of eligible soil-preparation and planting contractors. I translate the broad prevalence of older owners into five-year succession pressure, then discount for multi-owner firms, internal family succession, farm-affiliated side businesses, equipment auctions, closures, and asset sales without a transferable customer operation. Agricultural contractors may skew older and family-owned, but capital intensity and larger operating scale can also support professional management.
caveats: The Census age evidence is cross-industry, represents responding employer-business owners, and uses 2018 data.
Equipment and customer lists can transfer without acquisition of the legal operating company.
Owner age is not a direct measure of sale intent, timing, or control-transfer type.
s5.change_evidence: Match the eligible universe to beneficial-owner age, ownership tenure, successor involvement, equipment-finance maturity, customer concentration, auction history, and transaction records; interview agricultural lenders, equipment dealers, auctioneers, and regional custom-operator associations.
s5.rationale: Equipment-intensive local contractors often build customer routes and reputation over many years, creating succession potential as owners age. A qualifying transfer is less frequent than retirement because some operators sell machinery piecemeal, fold work into a related farm, pass it within the family, or close rather than transfer customer relationships and staff.

principal_driver: Contractors can spread expensive precision equipment and supervisory technology across many customer acres during labor-constrained seasonal windows.
principal_weakness: Large farms can use the same automation to self-perform, while transparent per-acre pricing and local competition constrain durable retention.

sources.supports: Defines 115112 services as crop-production support including crop dusting and spraying, plowing, fertilizer application, seeding, planting, thinning, transplanting, pollinating, cultivating, and weed or pest control, while distinguishing adjacent support industries.
sources.title: 2022 Economic Census Classification Form: Agricultural Services

sources.supports: Provides the broader support-activities occupation mix, including 67.20 percent farming occupations, 13.78 percent transportation and material moving, and detailed agricultural equipment operator, management, and office employment.
sources.title: May 2023 OEWS Industry-Specific Occupational Employment and Wage Estimates: NAICS 115100

sources.supports: Reports increasing farm reliance on outside agricultural services and long-run inflation-adjusted receipt growth alongside fewer service establishments and fewer farms.
sources.title: The Growing Contribution of Support Services to U.S. Agricultural Production

sources.supports: Reports 2023 use of guidance auto-steering by 52 percent of midsize and 70 percent of large-scale crop farms, substantial mapping use, size-linked adoption, and labor-time savings as an adoption motive.
sources.title: Precision Agriculture Use Increases With Farm Size and Varies Widely by Technology

sources.supports: Documents guidance coverage of major row-crop acreage, automated steering for planting, tilling, fertilizing, and harvesting, reduced operator attention, input efficiency, adoption barriers, and connectivity needs.
sources.title: Most Row Crop Acreage Managed Using Auto-Steer and Guidance Systems

sources.supports: Reports surveyed per-acre minimum, maximum, median, and average rates for plowing, disking, cultivating, field preparation, strip tillage, and planting, illustrating fixed job pricing and dispersion.
sources.title: 2025 Custom Work Charges in Maryland and Delaware

sources.supports: Reports a 7 percent decline in farm count and a 2.2 percent decline in land in farms from 2017 to 2022, with average farm size increasing.
sources.title: 2022 Census of Agriculture: Number of U.S. Farms Falls Below 2 Million

sources.supports: Reports that 51 percent of responding US employer-business owners were at least age 55, based on the 2019 Annual Business Survey using 2018 data.
sources.title: Business Owners' Ages

sources.supports: Reports that cover-crop acreage increased 17 percent from 2017 to 2022, evidence that service passes can change even when total cropland does not grow.
sources.title: 2022 Census of Agriculture: Cover Crop Use Continues To Be Most Common in Eastern United States

title: Soil Preparation, Planting, and Cultivating
